draft.introduction: The baptism of Jesus by John in the Jordan. The heavens open, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Father’s voice declares “This is my beloved Son”—the first trinitarian theophany in the Gospels.

translation.text: Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to John, to be baptized by him. John would have prevented him, saying, “I need to be baptized by you, and do you come to me?” But Jesus answered him, “Let it be so now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness.” And when Jesus was baptized, immediately he went up from the water, and behold, the heavens were opened to him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove. And a voice from heaven said, “This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.”
translation.original_text: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός ἐν ὡ εὐδόκησα

translator_notes.note: The voice echoes Psalm 2:7 (“You are my Son”) and Isaiah 42:1 (“my chosen, in whom my soul delights”). Matthew changes Mark’s “You are” to “This is,” making the declaration public rather than private—a proclamation to the world, not just to Jesus.
